Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I tried to set aside about 20 hours a week, but with my low resources situation, I often had to work part-time jobs that cut into my study time. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to create diagrams and charts when I could, but organizing a proper routine was challenging. I'd usually end up studying in bursts whenever I could find quiet time, which wasn't always predictable.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch my coursework because of work or other responsibilities, then I'd have intense catch-up sessions before something was due. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but the reality is that my attendance being around 48% reflects how unpredictable things got. Deadlines were really the main driver of when I'd actually sit down and focus.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was checking if there were new assignments posted or if grades had come back. I'd also log in when I remembered there was a video I needed to watch for an upcoming deadline. Sometimes I'd get an email notification that would remind me to check in. It wasn't really a habit—more like a reactive thing when I knew I had to do something specific.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more routine, especially early in the semester when I was trying to stay on top of things. But honestly, that didn't last long. My anxiety would sometimes make me avoid logging in because I was afraid of seeing what I'd missed or fallen behind on. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped a bit with that fear, but I wouldn't say the LMS ever became a natural daily habit for me.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I could pause them, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I also found the assignment rubrics helpful because they gave me a clear picture of what was expected. Anything that let me see information laid out visually rather than just reading dense text worked well for my learning style.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums entirely—I think I only posted like four times the whole course. I felt awkward jumping into conversations, and with zero peer interactions, it just felt uncomfortable. I also delayed reading materials that were just walls of text without any visual elements. When resources felt overwhelming or not immediately relevant to an assignment, I'd push them aside and tell myself I'd get to them later, which often didn't happen.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to my grade or to understanding something I'd need for an exam. If I could visualize the payoff—like knowing this video would help me understand a concept for the upcoming test—I'd engage with it. Activities that felt vague or disconnected from assessments were harder to prioritize, especially when I was already stressed and had limited time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. With my high anxiety and medium stress levels, I was constantly triaging what absolutely had to get done versus what would be nice to do. Required activities had this weight to them that made me push through even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff, even if interesting, got cut when time was tight, which was most of the time.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and videos when I had wifi access, then study them later when I didn't have reliable internet or was somewhere without connection. I also used my own notes heavily—I'd convert lecture content into visual diagrams and mind maps that I'd study from repeatedly. Textbooks from the library were another big resource that wouldn't show up anywhere in the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely learning but it wouldn't show in the system at all. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten diagrams and visual notes that I'd created from downloaded materials. Sometimes I'd study with a friend from class, going over concepts together, but none of that peer interaction happened through the LMS. My best learning often happened away from my computer, just me with my notes and drawings.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd access a video or reading once, download it if possible, and then work with it offline multiple times. With my limited resources, I couldn't always count on having consistent internet access, so downloading and working offline became my strategy. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times on my own.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engagement became really hard. My anxiety would spike, especially around midterms, and I'd find myself avoiding the LMS because logging in felt overwhelming. When I had competing responsibilities—work, family stuff—the course would slip. The mindfulness intervention actually helped during one of these rough patches; it gave me tools to manage the anxiety enough to at least check in and see what I needed to do.
Q13: All of those factors affected my LMS use significantly. Fatigue was a big one—after working, I'd be too tired to engage meaningfully even if I logged in. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules because I didn't know where to start. When workload piled up, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS and focus on just completing assignments rather than exploring additional resources. It became survival mode.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too exhausted to focus. I'd scroll through readings without really processing them. My anxiety sometimes made it hard to concentrate even when I was technically 'accessing' materials. The LMS would log my time there, but the quality of that engagement was pretty low during those moments.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting tasks feel really daunting—like there was this mental barrier I had to push through. Sometimes I'd avoid an assignment because I was confused about it and didn't want to face that confusion. Other times, I was just overwhelmed by everything on my plate and procrastination felt like the only way to cope in the moment, even though it made things worse later.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without a deadline looming, I'd struggle to engage with materials at all. As deadlines approached, I'd have these intense bursts of activity where I'd log in frequently and try to catch up on everything. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was what got me to actually complete assignments. My assignment completion rate of about 52% shows that even deadlines weren't always enough when things got too overwhelming.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though sometimes in complicated ways. Positive feedback would give me a temporary boost and make me more likely to engage with the next assignment. But critical feedback, especially if I didn't understand it, could increase my anxiety and make me want to avoid the course for a bit. I appreciated detailed feedback that showed me visually what I needed to improve—that matched my learning style better.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact. When modules were clearly laid out with visual roadmaps of what to do and when, I found it easier to participate. But when the structure felt confusing or when there were too many small tasks scattered around, I'd lose track and fall behind. Having 13 online courses meant I was juggling a lot, so clear structure in each course was essential for me to keep up.
Q19: Visual elements in the course design definitely made engagement easier—infographics, video content, clearly organized pages. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when navigation felt confusing. Also, required peer interactions were tough for me since I had basically zero peer interactions recorded. The design assumed a level of social engagement that didn't come naturally to me, especially with my anxiety.
Q20: I think that pattern might reflect students who are trying to figure things out but struggling to actually produce work. They might be logging in, watching videos, reading materials, but when it comes to assignments, something blocks them—maybe confusion, anxiety, or perfectionism. I've had moments like that where I'd spend hours on the LMS trying to understand something but still couldn't translate that into a completed assignment.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. I managed to get a Merit grade and decent exam scores despite low LMS engagement because a lot of my real learning happened offline. I'd download materials, create my own visual study aids, and prepare for exams using methods that didn't register in the system. The LMS activity doesn't capture studying from printed notes or reviewing diagrams I drew myself.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when students log in, how long they spend on videos, and whether they submit work. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually focused or just has a tab open. They miss offline studying entirely, which for someone like me with limited resources is huge. They also can't capture the emotional experience—the anxiety, the effort it takes just to log in some days.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious and want to learn more, when I'm actively thinking about concepts rather than passively consuming content. For me, real engagement often looks like creating visual representations of ideas, connecting new information to what I already know. It's a mental state more than just an activity count.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much the mindfulness intervention helped me manage my anxiety enough to keep going. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials away from my computer, or the visual notes I created that were my main study tool. It also wouldn't show the emotional labor of pushing through anxiety to engage at all, or how surprised I was when I actually did better than I expected on exams despite my struggles.
